examples: Chris Retzler has built a prototype of a machine called the wave rotor.
This minuscule rotor wiggled clockwise or anticlockwise as molecules bumped into it from one side or the other.
As it does, the rotor gains energy.
Exhibits include an eight-foot-long rotor, half of a hundred-and-fifty-ton truck, and a pump of Brobdingnagian proportions.
Its Radmax engine uses two wave-shaped cams that rotate above and below a stationary rotor.
